case_name: Baptist Health Medical Group, Inc. v. Carla Wellman

summary.brief: Baptist Health Medical Group, Inc. v. Carla Wellman. Medical malpractice case involving cardiology.

evidence_index.text: David by multiple providers employed by Baptist Health for a complex presentation of health issues, including weight-related cardiac problems, respiratory issues, heart disease, congestive heart failure, obstructive sleep apnea, hyperlipidemia, hypertension, morbid obesity, COPD, and diabetes.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Upon arrival at Baptist Health’s emergency room on August 22, 2017, David was lethargic and unable to communicate. Due to respiratory failure, he was immediately placed on a ventilator.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: After David was admitted, Dr. Manchi performed a transesophageal echocardiogram (TEE) and noted that although it was a 'technically difficult study,' he did not believe evidence of vegetation or endocarditis was present.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: David suffered a brain bleed and his anti-coagulant medication had to be reversed. He was subsequently transferred to the University of Louisville Hospital where physicians conducted further testing, including an additional TEE which indicated several lesions on the mitral valve and which were believed to be thrombus rather than vegetation.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: David passed away while at the University of Louisville Hospital.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Frizzell concluded that Baptist Health and Dr. Manchi had 'failed to meet the applicable standard of care as to the reading of the TEE.'
evidence_index.speaker: Expert witness

evidence_index.text: We conclude that Baptist Health’s designated evidence created a genuine issue of material fact as to whether Dr. Manchi’s treatment of David complied with the standard of care to preclude the entry of summary judgment in favor of Wellman.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: Our review is limited to those facts designated to the trial court, and we construe all facts and reasonable inferences drawn from those facts in favor of the non-moving party.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: David presented with a complex presentation of health issues, including weight-related cardiac problems, respiratory issues, heart disease, congestive heart failure, obstructive sleep apnea, hyperlipidemia, hypertension, morbid obesity, COPD, and diabetes.
initial_state.initial_working_problem: Acute decompensation in a patient with complex cardiac and respiratory history

requestables.name: Transesophageal Echocardiogram (TEE)
requestables.description: Imaging to assess the mitral valve for vegetation or thrombus

reveal.result_summary: Technically difficult study, no evidence of vegetation or endocarditis
reveal.decision_impact: Led to continued medical therapy including antibiotics

requestables.name: Follow-up TEE at University of Louisville Hospital
requestables.description: Repeat imaging to reassess mitral valve

reveal.result_summary: Several lesions on the mitral valve, believed to be thrombus
reveal.decision_impact: Contributed to the decision to reverse anticoagulation

timeline_phases.name: Emergency Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents to Baptist Health's emergency room

key_events.event: Patient arrives at ED, lethargic and unable to communicate

timeline_phases.name: Initial Workup and Management
timeline_phases.description: Initial assessment and management including TEE

timeline_phases.name: Transfer and Further Testing
timeline_phases.description: Patient transferred to University of Louisville Hospital for further testing

key_events.event: Repeat TEE at University of Louisville Hospital

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient with mechanical mitral valve and risk of endocarditis
decision_points.prompt: How should the TEE findings be interpreted and managed?

options.description: Interpret TEE as negative for endocarditis and continue current management
options.clinical_reasoning: Initial TEE interpretation did not show vegetation

options.description: Consider repeat imaging or additional studies to rule out endocarditis
options.clinical_reasoning: Standard of care requires thorough investigation to rule out endocarditis

actual_action_defendant.description: Continued medical therapy including antibiotics based on initial TEE interpretation
actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Believed no evidence of vegetation or endocarditis

expected_action_court.description: Further investigation should have been conducted to rule out endocarditis
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence indicated that the standard of care was not met

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to meet the applicable standard of care increased the risk of death from infective endocarditis
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Further studies should have been conducted to rule out endocarditis
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by expert testimony

patient_outcome.description: Patient suffered a brain bleed and passed away

key_findings: Genuine issue of material fact regarding standard of care
Summary judgment reversed and remanded for further proceedings

malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Further investigation could have potentially altered outcome

factual_timeline.event: Patient arrives at Baptist Health's emergency room

tests_performed.result: No evidence of vegetation or endocarditis

diagnoses.when_made: After transfer to University of Louisville Hospital

court_findings.finding: Genuine issue of material fact regarding standard of care
